game_description: Drop discs to connect four in a row before your opponent, 2 players.
filter_1_label: Did you go first this game?

strategies.strategy_detailed: Connect 4 is solved (Allis 1988) — P1 wins only by opening column 4 (center). Per solver databases: cols 3 or 5 draw with perfect P2 play; cols 1, 2, 6, or 7 lose to perfect P2. Center participates in more four-in-a-row lines than any other column.

P2 cannot win against perfect play — draw is the ceiling once P1 opens center. Solver-confirmed drawing replies: stack center (col 4) or play adjacent (cols 3 or 5). Cols 1, 2, 6, 7 all lose against perfect P1 follow-up. Play for the draw; no comeback line exists.

Build threats in two directions from a shared disc — horizontal and diagonal. Threats must be playable: both extension cells need support underneath (gravity rule). Three in a row with two playable extensions is a forced win — opponent can only block one end.

P2's path to draw is claim-even: occupy threats on even-numbered rows. Every disc should advance an even-row threat or block P1's odd-row threat. Pure blocking without parity progress cedes tempo to P1 and accelerates the forced loss.

strategies.condition: Opponent must respond to your threat
strategies.strategy_detailed: A forced response (opponent must block or lose) lets you build elsewhere. Chain forced responses: after they block, create a new threat in a different area. Three consecutive forced responses often result in a position where you have two simultaneous winning moves.
strategy_stealth: Chain forced blocks in different areas

strategies.strategy_detailed: Claim-even rule (Allis): in zugzwang endgames, P1 wins threats on odd rows (1, 3, 5); P2 wins threats on even rows (2, 4, 6). When forcing players to fill columns, the threat sitting on your parity row triggers; opponent's sitting on theirs does not. Plan your winning row to match your parity.

strategies.condition: Board filling with no winner yet
strategies.strategy_detailed: On a filling board, zugzwang (any move you make loses) becomes a real threat. Avoid building columns where your own disc creates a winning row for the opponent above it. Before each move, check: does this disc set up opponent's winning position one row above?
strategy_stealth: Check: does this disc set up their win above?
Avoid columns with opponent win above

strategies.strategy_detailed: Two simultaneous threats (a 'double threat') guarantee a win — opponent can block only one. Create a double threat by building a single disc that completes two different potential four-in-a-rows. If your opponent cannot block both, play the double threat move immediately.
strategy_stealth: One disc completing two different fours
Play it the moment it appears

strategies.condition: Board nearly full, you hold tempo
strategies.strategy_detailed: With 6-8 slots remaining and tempo advantage, count the forced sequence. If your forced win requires opponent to fill a specific column in a specific order, verify they have no alternative that blocks your win along the way. Calculate 3 moves deep before committing.
strategy_stealth: Verify opponent has no blocking alternative
Calculate before committing — not by feel

strategies.strategy_detailed: Once P1 has a forced win line, a drawn full board is your ceiling. Apply claim-even: play to fill even rows yourself (your parity) and force P1's threats onto even rows (their wrong parity). A drawn final board (no four-in-a-row anywhere) is P2's win condition.
strategy_stealth: Force P1 threats to even rows
